Aripiprazole was effective for irritability (ABC-I) and hyperactivity vs placebo in two primary RCTs (Marcus 2009, Owen 2009). Adverse effects included somnolence, weight gain (~1-1.5 kg), sedation, and drooling. All three trials funded by Bristol-Myers Squibb and Otsuka Pharmaceutical.
mechanism_of_action: Third-generation atypical antipsychotic. Acts as a partial agonist at D2 and D3 dopamine receptors (stabilizes dopamine activity — agonizes when dopamine is low, antagonizes when high). Partial agonist at 5-HT1A serotonin receptors; antagonist at 5-HT2A. Unique mechanism differs from typical D2 blockers: dopamine system stabilizer. Does not cause hyperprolactinemia.
sub_mechanism: Partial D2/D3 agonist; 5-HT1A partial agonist; 5-HT2A antagonist (dopamine system stabilizer)
results_summary: Both primary RCTs showed significant ABC-Irritability reductions vs placebo: Marcus 2009 (n=98, ages 6-17): ABC-I -14.4 vs -8.4 (p<0.05); Owen 2009 (n=218, ages 6-17): ABC-I -12.4 vs -5.0 (p<0.05). Weight gain ~1.5 kg > placebo. Significantly less prolactin elevation than risperidone. Response rate ~50% for aripiprazole vs 14% placebo.

title: Aripiprazole in acute treatment of children and adolescents with autism spectrum disorder: a systematic review and meta-analysis
authors: Maneeton B, Srisurapanont M, Maneeton N, Likhitsathian S, Suttajit S, Putthisri S
abstract_summary: Systematic review and meta-analysis of aripiprazole RCTs in children/adolescents with ASD. Analyzed 3-4 RCTs demonstrating aripiprazole superiority over placebo for irritability (ABC-I), hyperactivity, and stereotypies. Effect sizes moderate to large; somnolence, weight gain, and sedation main adverse effects. Fixed doses (5, 10, 15 mg/day) and flexible doses (2-15 mg/day) both effective.
mechanism_of_action: Partial D2/D3 dopamine receptor agonist and 5-HT2A antagonist with partial 5-HT1A agonism. Functions as a dopamine system stabilizer. Low intrinsic activity at D2 receptors means it reduces hyperdopaminergic states without causing the full receptor blockade adverse effects (EPS, hyperprolactinemia) of traditional antipsychotics.
sub_mechanism: Partial D2/D3 agonist; dopamine system stabilizer
results_summary: Meta-analysis confirms aripiprazole significant on ABC-I vs placebo (pooled SMD approximately -0.60 to -0.80). Somnolence (32-38%), weight gain (average 1.3-1.5 kg vs placebo), sedation main adverse effects. Effect sizes comparable to risperidone for irritability.

title: Pharmacotherapy of Autism Spectrum Disorder: Results from the Randomized BAART Clinical Trial
authors: DeVane CL, Charles JM, Abramson R, Williams JE, Carpenter L, Raven S, et al.
abstract_summary: Randomized double-blind parallel-group head-to-head RCT (N=61 randomized, ages 6-17) comparing aripiprazole vs risperidone for autism. Both arms showed significant ABC-Irritability improvement beginning at week 1 and continuing through 22 weeks. Risperidone showed greater improvement at weeks 3 and 6 (p<0.05). Weight gain was significantly greater with risperidone at weeks 4 and 10, but difference resolved at the optional 12-week extension endpoint.
mechanism_of_action: Risperidone: D2/5-HT2A antagonist; Aripiprazole: Partial D2/5-HT1A agonist/5-HT2A antagonist. Trial design allows direct comparison of D2 full blockade vs partial agonism approaches to ASD irritability management.
sub_mechanism: D2 full antagonism (risperidone) vs D2 partial agonism (aripiprazole) — direct comparison
results_summary: Both drugs significantly improved ABC-Irritability from week 1 onward. Risperidone greater improvement at weeks 3 and 6 (p<0.05). Weight gain: risperidone 3.31 kg vs aripiprazole 1.61 kg at week 10 (p<0.001); difference non-significant at 22-week extension. Both FDA-approved options supported.

title: Lurasidone for the Treatment of Irritability Associated with Autistic Disorder
authors: Loebel A, Brams M, Goldman RS, Silva R, Hernandez D, Deng L, Mankoski R, Findling RL
journal: Journal of Autism and Developmental Disorders
abstract_summary: Multicenter double-blind RCT (N=150, ages 6-17) testing lurasidone 20 mg/day vs 60 mg/day vs placebo for 6 weeks. Primary endpoint (ABC-Irritability) was not significantly different between lurasidone and placebo at week 6 (-8.8, -9.4 vs -7.5; p=0.55 and 0.36). Lurasidone 20 mg showed significant CGI-I improvement (p=0.035). Study failed its primary endpoint. Common adverse effects: vomiting (18%), somnolence (12%).
mechanism_of_action: Atypical antipsychotic with high affinity D2 antagonism (Ki 1.68 nM), 5-HT2A antagonism (Ki 2.03 nM), 5-HT7 antagonism (Ki 0.495 nM), and partial 5-HT1A agonism (Ki 6.74 nM). Also antagonizes alpha-2C and alpha-2A adrenergic receptors. 5-HT7 antagonism may be relevant to circadian regulation and mood. Low affinity for histamine H1 and muscarinic receptors (favorable metabolic profile).
results_summary: Failed primary endpoint: ABC-I not significantly improved vs placebo (lurasidone 20 mg p=0.55; 60 mg p=0.36). CGI-I significant for 20 mg only (p=0.035). No significant effects on ABC subscales (hyperactivity, stereotypy, social withdrawal). Modest weight/metabolic changes. Well tolerated. Lurasidone not established as efficacious for ASD irritability in this trial.

title: Lurasidone an Effective Alternative for the Treatment of Irritability Associated With Autism Spectrum Disorder (Case Series)
abstract_summary: Case series of 4 pediatric/adolescent inpatients with ASD and challenging behaviors treated with lurasidone due to failure or intolerance of risperidone/aripiprazole. All four showed improvement in irritability and behavioral management with lurasidone, suggesting a role as an alternative after first-line antipsychotic failure. Reinforces need for controlled trials at flexible dosing.
mechanism_of_action: D2/5-HT2A/5-HT7 antagonist; partial 5-HT1A agonist. Favorable metabolic profile vs risperidone (minimal weight gain, prolactin-sparing). 5-HT7 antagonism may improve circadian rhythm disruptions common in ASD.
results_summary: All 4 cases showed improvement on lurasidone after prior antipsychotic failures/intolerances. Weight gain and metabolic changes minimal. Authors conclude lurasidone is a viable alternative for treatment-resistant ASD irritability requiring further controlled study.

title: Review of the Pharmacotherapy of Irritability of Autism (Haloperidol & other antipsychotics)
journal: J Can Acad Child Adolesc Psychiatry
abstract_summary: Comprehensive narrative review of RCT data on pharmacotherapy of autism irritability. Reviews haloperidol vs clomipramine vs placebo (Remington 2001, n=36) — haloperidol superior on CARS and ABC-I. Reviews risperidone vs haloperidol (Miral 2008, n=30) — risperidone superior on ABC. Documents haloperidol historical efficacy but higher extrapyramidal side effects vs atypicals. Also covers olanzapine, aripiprazole, and risperidone data.
mechanism_of_action: Haloperidol: First-generation typical antipsychotic. High-affinity D2 receptor antagonist (also blocks D3, D4 receptors and sigma receptors). Reduces dopaminergic neurotransmission in mesolimbic (behavioral effects) and mesocortical pathways. Higher EPS risk than atypicals due to potent D2 blockade in nigrostriatal pathways. Little serotonergic activity (contrast with atypicals).
sample_size: Multiple trials reviewed (n=30-36 per haloperidol study)
results_summary: Remington 2001 (n=36): Haloperidol superior to clomipramine and placebo (CARS: -5.1 vs -4.0 vs -2.4; ABC-I: -7.0 vs -3.0 vs -2.0). Miral 2008 (n=30): Risperidone superior to haloperidol (ABC: -48.8 vs -21.3). Haloperidol adverse effects: fatigue (38%), dystonia (7%), weight gain similar to risperidone (~4.6 kg). Olanzapine RCT (n=11): CGI-I significant vs placebo but non-significant CY-BOCS. Historical role established; atypicals now preferred due to better tolerability.

title: Impact of antipsychotics in children and adolescents with autism spectrum disorder: a systematic review and meta-analysis
authors: De Crescenzo F, Valeri G, Ficco L, et al. (multiple authors)
journal: Health and Quality of Life Outcomes
abstract_summary: Systematic review and meta-analysis (Italian national guideline support) of RCTs comparing antipsychotics vs placebo in children/adolescents with ASD. Antipsychotics significantly reduced stereotypies, hyperactivity, irritability, and obsessive/compulsive behaviors vs placebo; increased social communication and global functioning. More acceptable but less safe (metabolic, neurological adverse effects) than placebo.
mechanism_of_action: Class-level review: Second-generation antipsychotics block D2 receptors and serotonin 5-HT2A receptors. The combination of D2 blockade reducing mesolimbic dopamine excess (implicated in ASD behavioral dysregulation) with 5-HT2A antagonism modulating prefrontal dopamine release forms the basis for behavioral improvement in ASD.
study_type: Systematic review and meta-analysis (national guideline analysis)
results_summary: Antipsychotics significantly efficacious vs placebo for stereotypies, hyperactivity, irritability, obsessions, and overall behavior in pediatric ASD. Significant improvement in social communication and global functioning. More acceptable but metabolic risks (weight gain, dyslipidemia) and neurological AEs (EPS, tardive dyskinesia risk) documented.

title: Pharmacological intervention for irritability, aggression, and self-injury in autism spectrum disorder (ASD) — Cochrane Review
authors: Gillies D, Iffland M, Jorgensen M, Livingstone N, Hazell P
abstract_summary: Comprehensive Cochrane review of pharmacological interventions for irritability, aggression, and self-injury in ASD (searched to June 2022). Atypical antipsychotics showed significant reductions in irritability vs placebo with moderate-quality evidence. ADHD medications reduced irritability slightly (SMD -0.20, 10 studies, N=400). Metabolic adverse effects documented with antipsychotics. Neurohormones showed uncertain evidence.
drug_name: Multiple (risperidone, aripiprazole, ADHD medications, and others)
mechanism_of_action: Comprehensive review covering D2/5-HT2A antagonists (antipsychotics), norepinephrine reuptake inhibitors (atomoxetine), alpha-2 agonists (guanfacine/clonidine), and stimulants (methylphenidate). Each class targets different aspects of ASD behavioral dysregulation through modulation of monoaminergic neurotransmission.
sample_size: Multiple RCTs (400+ participants for ADHD medications; hundreds more for antipsychotics)
results_summary: Atypical antipsychotics reduced irritability with moderate certainty. ADHD medications (including methylphenidate, atomoxetine, guanfacine) reduced irritability slightly (SMD -0.20, 10 RCTs, N=400). Metabolic AEs common with antipsychotics. Very uncertain evidence for most other drug classes.

title: Acetylcholinesterase inhibitors for autistic spectrum disorders — Cochrane Review
authors: Ure A, Cox GR, Haslam R, Williams K
abstract_summary: Cochrane review of AChEI RCTs in autism (2 RCTs included, N=74 total). Galantamine+risperidone vs placebo+risperidone (n=40, ages 4-12): small improvement in irritability (MD -3.50, 95% CI -6.39 to -0.61); no significant difference in social communication or repetitive behaviors; higher adverse events with galantamine (75% vs 35%). Donepezil vs placebo (n=34, ages 8-17): no significant differences on core autism features or irritability. Both findings very low-certainty evidence. Rivastigmine: no RCTs found.
mechanism_of_action: Acetylcholinesterase inhibitors (AChEIs) block the enzyme acetylcholinesterase, preventing breakdown of acetylcholine, thereby increasing cholinergic neurotransmission. Relevant in ASD due to documented reductions in brain acetylcholine, decreased nicotinic and muscarinic receptor expression (especially in parietal and temporal cortex), and reduced cholinergic basal forebrain projections in ASD neuropathology. Galantamine additionally acts as a positive allosteric modulator of nicotinic acetylcholine receptors (nAChRs), which may provide additional cognitive and behavioral benefits.
sub_mechanism: Acetylcholinesterase inhibitor; nicotinic receptor positive allosteric modulator (galantamine only)
results_summary: Galantamine+risperidone: significant irritability reduction (MD -3.50, p<0.05), but no significant improvement in social communication or repetitive behaviors. Adverse events higher with galantamine (OR 5.57). Donepezil: no benefit on any primary or secondary outcomes; hyperactivity potentially worsened (MD +2.60). Evidence overall very low-certainty. No RCT data for rivastigmine in ASD.

title: Galantamine efficacy and tolerability as an augmentative therapy in autistic children: A randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial
authors: Ghaleiha A, Ghyasvand M, Mohammadi M-R, Farokhnia M, Yadegari N, Tabrizi M, Hajiaghaee R, Yekehtaz H, Akhondzadeh S
abstract_summary: Double-blind, placebo-controlled RCT of galantamine as adjunct to risperidone in autistic children. Galantamine group demonstrated significantly greater improvement than placebo on ABC-Irritability (p=0.017) and ABC-Lethargy/Social Withdrawal (p=0.005) subscales. Adverse event frequency comparable between groups. Concluded galantamine augmentation was relatively effective and safe for alleviating autism-related behavioral symptoms.
mechanism_of_action: Selective, reversible, competitive acetylcholinesterase inhibitor — prevents acetylcholine breakdown and increases synaptic ACh concentrations. Uniquely also acts as a positive allosteric modulator (PAM) at nicotinic acetylcholine receptors (alpha-4-beta-2 and alpha-7 subtypes), enhancing nicotinic receptor sensitivity and activation. Addresses documented cholinergic deficit in ASD: reduced basal forebrain cholinergic projections, reduced cortical nicotinic and muscarinic receptor density, and lower ACh levels in autism brains.
sub_mechanism: Acetylcholinesterase inhibitor + nicotinic receptor positive allosteric modulator (PAM)
results_summary: Galantamine+risperidone significantly superior to placebo+risperidone on: ABC-Irritability (p=0.017) and ABC-Lethargy/Social Withdrawal (p=0.005). No significant differences on ABC-Stereotypy, Hyperactivity, or Inappropriate Speech. Adverse event frequencies similar between groups. Concluded effective and safe as augmentative therapy.

title: Safety and Efficacy of Donepezil in Children and Adolescents with Autism: Neuropsychological Measures
authors: Handen BL, Johnson CR, McAuliffe-Bellin S, Murray PJ, Hardan AY
journal: Journal of Child and Adolescent Psychopharmacology
abstract_summary: Double-blind, placebo-controlled 10-week RCT of donepezil (5-10 mg/day) in 34 children/adolescents (ages 8-17) with ASD (IQ >75) assessing executive functioning (EF). No statistically significant between-group differences on any neuropsychological measure. Both groups improved within study period. Followed by 10-week open-label phase for placebo non-responders showing modest gains on sorting tasks only. Donepezil well tolerated with minor side effects.
mechanism_of_action: Selective, reversible acetylcholinesterase inhibitor (AChEI). Inhibits AChE predominantly in the CNS, increasing synaptic acetylcholine availability in cortical and hippocampal regions. Rationale in ASD: post-mortem studies show reduced nicotinic and muscarinic receptor density in parietal/temporal cortex, reduced basal forebrain cholinergic neuron counts, and decreased choline acetyltransferase activity in autism brains. Hypothesized to improve executive functioning, attention, and social cognition via enhanced cholinergic neurotransmission.
study_type: Double-blind randomized placebo-controlled trial + open-label extension
results_summary: No significant between-group differences on any EF measure (D-KEFS Trail Making, Verbal/Design Fluency, etc.) or behavioral outcomes. Both groups showed within-group improvements. Open-label phase: significant gains only on DKEFS Sorting Test (STCC p=0.004, STFS p=0.001 vs baseline). Donepezil well tolerated (minor diarrhea, headache). Insufficient evidence to support donepezil for EF in ASD.

title: The role of nicotinic acetylcholine receptors in the pathophysiology and pharmacotherapy of autism spectrum disorder: Focus on α7 nicotinic receptors
authors: Oz M, Kury LA, Sadek B, Mahgoub M
journal: International Journal of Biochemistry & Cell Biology
abstract_summary: Comprehensive review of nicotinic acetylcholine receptor (nAChR) involvement in ASD pathophysiology and therapeutic potential. Documents: (1) post-mortem brain evidence of reduced alpha-7 nAChR in ASD; (2) CHRNA7 gene deletions at 15q13.3 linked to ASD; (3) preclinical and clinical evidence for alpha-7 nAChR agonists as therapeutic targets; (4) roles of nAChRs in cognition, neuroinflammation, excitation/inhibition balance, and social behavior relevant to ASD.
drug_name: Alpha-7 nicotinic receptor agonists (including nicotine, EVP-6124/encenicline, GTS-21/DMXBA)
mechanism_of_action: Alpha-7 nicotinic acetylcholine receptors (α7-nAChRs) are ligand-gated ion channels (cation channels) distributed in hippocampus, prefrontal cortex, and brain regions critical for social cognition and learning. Activation: (1) increases presynaptic ACh, glutamate, and GABA release; (2) modulates NMDA receptor activity; (3) reduces neuroinflammation via vagal anti-inflammatory pathway; (4) improves excitation/inhibition balance disrupted in ASD; (5) enhances cognitive functions (attention, working memory). CHRNA7 (encoding alpha-7 nAChR) deletions at 15q13.3 are among the most common CNV findings in ASD, providing genetic rationale for alpha-7 agonism as therapy.
results_summary: Preclinical data: alpha-7 nAChR agonists (GTS-21, PNU-282987) rescue social deficits in VPA and BTBR mouse models. Clinical trials limited: GTS-21 showed modest cognitive improvement in small ASD pilot studies. Alpha-7 agonists remain promising but lack Phase 3 trial data for ASD. Alpha-7 partial agonists (EVP-6124) showed cognitive benefits in Alzheimer's — trials in ASD warranted.

title: An Exploratory Trial of Transdermal Nicotine for Aggression and Irritability in Adults with Autism Spectrum Disorder
authors: Lewis AS, van Schalkwyk GI, Ortiz Lopez M, Volkmar FR, Picciotto MR, Sukhodolsky DG
journal: Journal of Autism and Developmental Disorders
abstract_summary: Exploratory double-blind crossover pilot RCT (N=8 adults with ASD) testing 7 mg transdermal nicotine vs placebo (one week each) for aggression and irritability. All subjects tolerated nicotine well. Five contributed ABC-Irritability data showing improvement favoring nicotine (not statistically significant due to small N: mean -12 vs -7, d=0.49). Sleep quality also improved with nicotine (d=0.88), correlating strongly with ABC-I improvement (r²=0.89, p=0.016). Two subjects continued open-label up to 21 mg with sustained benefit at 14-16 months.
mechanism_of_action: Nicotine is a non-selective agonist at nicotinic acetylcholine receptors (nAChRs), primarily activating alpha-7 and alpha-4-beta-2 subtypes. In ASD: targets documented alpha-7 nAChR deficiency (CHRNA7 gene deletions), modulates dopaminergic and serotonergic signaling, reduces neuroinflammation via alpha-7-mediated vagal anti-inflammatory pathway, and may improve sleep architecture. Transdermal delivery provides continuous low-level receptor stimulation avoiding phasic cigarette nicotine patterns.
sub_mechanism: Nicotinic acetylcholine receptor agonist (alpha-7 and alpha-4-beta-2 subtypes)
results_summary: Nicotine improved ABC-Irritability (not statistically significant, d=0.49), improved sleep quality significantly (p=0.13, d=0.88), and sleep improvement strongly correlated with irritability improvement (r²=0.89, p=0.016). 4/6 caregivers reported behavioral improvement. Well tolerated. Findings support further investigation of nAChR agonists for ASD aggression and sleep.

title: Brief Report: Initial Trial of Alpha7-Nicotinic Receptor Stimulation in Two Adult Patients with Autism Spectrum Disorder
authors: Olincy A, Blakeley-Smith A, Johnson L, Kem W, Freedman R
journal: Journal of Autism and Developmental Disorders
abstract_summary: First double-blind crossover pilot trial (N=2 adult ASD patients) of a selective alpha-7 nAChR partial agonist (DMXBA/GTS-21) vs placebo. Both patients showed increased self-reported attention and communication. Supports the hypothesis that alpha-7 nAChR agonists may reduce P50 sensory gating deficits and cognitive impairments in ASD. Provides proof-of-concept for future alpha-7-targeted trials in ASD.
drug_name: DMXBA (GTS-21) — Alpha-7 nicotinic receptor partial agonist
mechanism_of_action: DMXBA (3-(2,4-dimethoxybenzylidene)anabaseine) is a selective partial agonist at alpha-7 nicotinic acetylcholine receptors. Binding to alpha-7 nAChRs: (1) opens Ca2+ channels, activating downstream signaling cascades (MAPK, PI3K); (2) reduces neuroinflammation; (3) enhances presynaptic glutamate and GABA release; (4) normalizes P50 auditory gating (sensory filtering). Genetic rationale: CHRNA7 deletions at 15q13.3 strongly associated with ASD; direct partial agonism may compensate for receptor underexpression.
sub_mechanism: Selective alpha-7 nicotinic acetylcholine receptor partial agonist
results_summary: Both patients reported increased attention and communication. Objective measures showed sensory gating (P50 ratio) improvements. Proof-of-concept for alpha-7 partial agonist approach. Larger trials needed. Supports alpha-7 nAChR as therapeutic target in ASD given CHRNA7 genetic association.

title: Methylphenidate for children and adolescents with autism spectrum disorder (Cochrane Review)
authors: Sturman N, Deckx L, van Driel ML
abstract_summary: Cochrane review of methylphenidate for ASD (4 RCTs, N=113 children ages 5-13). High-dose methylphenidate (0.43-0.60 mg/kg/dose) significantly reduced hyperactivity as rated by teachers (SMD -0.78, 95% CI -1.13 to -0.43) and parents (MD -6.61). Inattention improved modestly by teacher rating only. Appetite suppression significantly higher with MPH (RR 8.28). No improvement in core ASD symptoms (social interaction, stereotypy). All 4 RCTs crossover design, 1-week treatment periods. Low/very low quality evidence overall.
mechanism_of_action: Central nervous system stimulant. Blocks reuptake of dopamine (DAT) and norepinephrine (NET) transporters in presynaptic terminals, increasing synaptic concentrations of dopamine and norepinephrine. In prefrontal cortex: norepinephrine stimulation of postsynaptic alpha-2A receptors strengthens PFC network connectivity, improving working memory and impulse control. In striatum: dopamine reuptake blockade reduces hyperactivity and impulsivity. Effects on ASD: targets dopaminergic and noradrenergic dysregulation contributing to ADHD-like symptoms (hyperactivity, inattention) in ASD comorbidity.
results_summary: Teacher-rated hyperactivity: SMD -0.78 (significant, clinically relevant). Parent-rated hyperactivity: MD -6.61 (significant). Teacher-rated inattention: MD -2.72 (borderline significant). No improvement in social interaction (SMD -0.51, NS) or stereotypy (SMD -0.34, NS). Decreased appetite RR 8.28 vs placebo. Withdrawal rate higher with MPH (trend). Studies limited to 1-week periods; long-term effects unknown.

title: Positive effects of methylphenidate on hyperactivity are moderated by monoaminergic gene variants in children with autism spectrum disorders
authors: Vitiello B, Shah B, McDougle CJ, Posey DJ, Tierney E, Nurmi EL, et al. (RUPP Autism Network)
abstract_summary: Pharmacogenomics study of the RUPP Autism Network's MPH crossover trial. Among 72 children with ASD, MPH significantly reduced hyperactivity (primary outcome). Genetic variants in DRD2, DRD3, SLC6A3 (DAT), and SLC6A4 (SERT) were associated with MPH response. DRD2 and DRD3 variants associated with tolerability/discontinuation. First MPH pharmacogenetics study in ASD, highlighting potential for precision dosing.
mechanism_of_action: Dopamine and norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor. The pharmacogenomic dimension: RUPP trial showed that DRD2 receptor polymorphisms, dopamine transporter gene variants (SLC6A3/DAT), and serotonin transporter variants (SLC6A4) all modulate MPH response in ASD. This indicates MPH effects in ASD are mediated through individualized dopaminergic and serotonergic system genetics, not simply a uniform pharmacological effect.
sub_mechanism: Dopamine/norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor — pharmacogenomics of response
study_type: Pharmacogenomic analysis of randomized crossover trial (RUPP)
results_summary: MPH significantly reduced hyperactivity in children with ASD (crossover RCT). DRD2 (p=0.001) and DRD3 (p=0.04) variants associated with tolerability/discontinuation. SLC6A3 (DAT) and SLC6A4 (SERT) variants moderated efficacy. 14 subjects discontinued due to adverse effects; DRD3 variants predictive of discontinuation. Supports precision medicine approach for MPH use in ASD.

title: Atomoxetine, Parent Training, and Their Combination in Children With Autism Spectrum Disorder and Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder
authors: Handen BL, Aman MG, Arnold LE, Hyman SL, Tumuluru RV, Lecavalier L, et al.
journal: Journal of the American Academy of Child & Adolescent Psychiatry
abstract_summary: 10-week, double-blind, 2×2 factorial RCT of atomoxetine (up to 1.8 mg/kg/day) ± parent training (PT) in 128 children (ages 5-14) with ASD and ADHD symptoms. Atomoxetine significantly reduced hyperactivity/impulsivity (primary outcome) vs placebo. PT provided modest additional benefit for noncompliance. Adverse events: decreased appetite (52% ATX vs 33% placebo, p=0.049) and fatigue were most common; well tolerated overall. Extension study confirmed 24-week durability.
mechanism_of_action: Selective norepinephrine (noradrenaline) reuptake inhibitor (SNRI/NRI). Blocks NET in prefrontal cortex and locus coeruleus, increasing synaptic norepinephrine. Secondarily increases dopamine in PFC (not striatum — minimal abuse potential). Norepinephrine acts on postsynaptic alpha-2A adrenergic receptors in PFC, strengthening PFC circuitry for impulse control, working memory, and attention. In ASD+ADHD: addresses the noradrenergic component of inattention and hyperactivity without D2 receptor effects of antipsychotics.
results_summary: Atomoxetine significantly reduced ADHD-RS Hyperactivity-Impulsivity (primary outcome) vs placebo. Parent training improved noncompliance but not ADHD core symptoms. Combined ATX+PT not significantly better than ATX alone for ADHD outcomes. Adverse events: decreased appetite (52% ATX), fatigue (44-50%), mood dysregulation lower with ATX+PT (28%) vs ATX alone (56%, p=0.04). 24-week extension (85% of responders) confirmed durable benefit.

title: Adverse Events of Atomoxetine in a Double-Blind Placebo-Controlled Study in Children with Autism
authors: Tumuluru RV, Corbett-Dick P, Aman MG, Smith T, Arnold LE, Pan X, et al.
journal: Journal of Child and Adolescent Psychopharmacology
abstract_summary: Safety analysis of the 128-child ATX RCT in ASD. Atomoxetine associated with decreased appetite (52% vs 33%, p=0.049) and fatigue (44-50%). Most adverse events resolved within 4 weeks. No QTc changes, serious cardiovascular events, or suicidal ideation (unlike TD children). Mood dysregulation lower when ATX combined with parent training vs ATX alone (28% vs 56%, p=0.04). Higher baseline adverse event rates in ASD children vs TD, emphasizing the need for ASD-specific safety data.
mechanism_of_action: Selective norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor. Blocks NET transporter in PFC and locus coeruleus. Increases synaptic norepinephrine and secondarily PFC dopamine (not striatal). The noradrenergic mechanism distinguishes atomoxetine from stimulants in terms of: (1) no abuse potential; (2) 24-hour coverage; (3) efficacy for both inattention and hyperactivity; (4) no QTc risk in ASD population (unlike some tricyclics used historically).
sub_mechanism: Selective norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor — safety analysis
study_type: Safety analysis of randomized placebo-controlled trial
results_summary: ATX: decreased appetite (52% vs 33%, p=0.049), fatigue (44-50%), mood dysregulation (lower with PT coadministration). Most AEs resolved within 4 weeks. No QTc, suicidality, or serious CV events. Baseline AE rates higher in ASD vs TD children. ATX safe profile established for ASD with dose up to 1.8 mg/kg/day.

title: Extended-Release Guanfacine for Hyperactivity in Children With Autism Spectrum Disorder
authors: Scahill L, McCracken JT, King BH, Rockhill CM, Shah B, Politte L, Sanders R, Minjarez M, Cowen J, Mullett J, Page C, Ward D, Deng Y, Loo S, Dziura J, McDougle CJ; RUPP Network
abstract_summary: Multisite double-blind RCT (N=62 children, ages 5-14 with ASD and moderate-severe hyperactivity) of extended-release guanfacine (GEXR) vs placebo for 8 weeks. GEXR produced 43.6% decline in ABC-Hyperactivity vs 13.2% for placebo (effect size=1.67, p<0.0001). Response rate (CGI-I much/very much improved): 50% GEXR vs 9.4% placebo. Significant ADHD-RS improvements (ES 1.17-2.03). Adverse effects: drowsiness (87%), fatigue (63%), decreased appetite (43%). No significant ECG changes. Blood pressure normalized by week 8.
mechanism_of_action: Selective alpha-2A adrenergic receptor agonist. Mechanism in PFC: guanfacine binds postsynaptic alpha-2A adrenergic receptors in prefrontal cortex, strengthening PFC network connections (reduces cAMP signaling via Gs, enhancing 'signal-to-noise' in PFC circuits). This improves working memory, impulse control, and reduces distractibility — specifically targeting the top-down PFC control deficits underlying ADHD symptoms. In locus coeruleus: presynaptic alpha-2A agonism reduces tonic norepinephrine release, decreasing arousal. Extended-release formulation provides steady plasma levels over 24 hours, reducing hemodynamic side effects vs immediate-release.
results_summary: ABC-Hyperactivity: 43.6% decline GEXR vs 13.2% placebo (LS means 19.3 vs 29.7, effect size 1.67, p<0.0001). ADHD-RS Inattention ES=1.17 (p=0.0001), Hyperactivity ES=1.72 (p<0.0001), Total ES=2.03 (p<0.0001). Response rate (CGI-I): 50% vs 9.4% (p<0.001). Adverse effects: drowsiness (87%), fatigue (63%), decreased appetite (43%). No ECG changes; BP declined early, returned to near-baseline by week 8. No cognitive impairment.

title: A randomized, placebo-controlled trial of extended-release guanfacine in children with autism spectrum disorder and ADHD symptoms: an analysis of secondary outcome measures
authors: Politte L, Scahill L, Figueroa J, McCracken J, King B, McDougle C
abstract_summary: Secondary outcomes analysis of the RUPP guanfacine XR ASD trial (N=62, same trial as Scahill 2015). GEXR showed significant improvements in parent-rated challenging behavior, repetitive behaviors, and sleep disturbance versus placebo as secondary outcomes. Provides additional evidence for GEXR's benefits beyond hyperactivity in ASD.
mechanism_of_action: Selective alpha-2A adrenergic receptor agonist (same as primary trial). Secondary outcomes suggest GEXR may modulate amygdalar reactivity (reducing challenging behaviors), serotoninergic-adrenergic sleep circuits (improving sleep disturbance), and cortico-striatal circuits controlling repetitive behaviors.
sub_mechanism: Selective alpha-2A adrenergic receptor agonist — secondary outcomes
results_summary: Significant improvements on secondary outcomes: Home Situations Questionnaire (parent-rated challenging behavior), ABC-Repetitive Behavior subscale, Children's Sleep Habits Questionnaire (sleep disturbance) — all significantly favoring GEXR vs placebo. Supports GEXR's broader beneficial effects beyond hyperactivity in ASD children.

title: Practitioner Review: Pharmacological treatment of ADHD symptoms in children and youth with autism spectrum disorder: a systematic review and meta-analysis
authors: Rodrigues R, Lai MC, Beswick A, Gorman D, Anagnostou E, Szatmari P, Anderson K, Ameis SH
journal: Journal of Child Psychology and Psychiatry
abstract_summary: Systematic review and meta-analysis of pharmacotherapy for ADHD symptoms in youth with ASD (25 RCTs). Methylphenidate (4 RCTs) reduced hyperactivity (parent SMD=-0.63; teacher SMD=-0.81) and inattention (parent SMD=-0.36; teacher SMD=-0.30). Atomoxetine (4 RCTs) reduced inattention (parent SMD=-0.54; teacher SMD=-0.38) and parent-rated hyperactivity (SMD=-0.49). One guanfacine RCT showed significant hyperactivity reduction. Overall quality of evidence low/very low. Supports MPH and ATX as effective for ADHD symptoms in ASD.
drug_name: Methylphenidate, Atomoxetine, Guanfacine (ADHD pharmacotherapy in ASD — systematic review)
mechanism_of_action: Methylphenidate: DAT/NET reuptake inhibitor (stimulant). Atomoxetine: selective NET inhibitor (non-stimulant). Guanfacine: alpha-2A adrenergic agonist (non-stimulant). All three target prefrontal cortex executive function circuits with different primary neurotransmitter profiles (dopaminergic+noradrenergic, primarily noradrenergic, and noradrenergic respectively).
sub_mechanism: ADHD pharmacotherapy meta-analysis across stimulant and non-stimulant classes
results_summary: Methylphenidate: hyperactivity parent SMD=-0.63, teacher SMD=-0.81; inattention parent SMD=-0.36, teacher SMD=-0.30. Atomoxetine: inattention parent SMD=-0.54, teacher SMD=-0.38; hyperactivity parent SMD=-0.49. Guanfacine (1 RCT): significant hyperactivity reduction (ES=1.67). Antipsychotics: indirect evidence for hyperactivity reduction. All evidence low/very-low quality. MPH and ATX have best evidence for ADHD symptoms in ASD.

title: Everolimus for epilepsy and autism spectrum disorder in tuberous sclerosis complex: EXIST-3 substudy in Japan
abstract_summary: A substudy of the phase 3 EXIST-3 trial examining everolimus in Japanese TSC patients with refractory epilepsy. Everolimus reduced seizure frequency vs. placebo, and a favorable trend was observed for ASD symptom improvement on PARS scores in 4 of 11 ASD patients receiving everolimus vs. 1 of 8 on placebo.
mechanism_of_action: mTORC1/mTORC2 inhibitor; blocks the mammalian target of rapamycin complex 1 by binding FKBP12; reduces hyperactivated mTOR signaling caused by TSC1/TSC2 mutations, normalizing synaptic protein synthesis, neuronal growth, and axonal myelination
target_symptoms: Seizures, ASD-related social and behavioral symptoms
results_summary: Response rate 30% (LE) and 28.6% (HE) vs. 0% placebo for seizures. Favorable trend in ASD symptom improvement; 4/11 ASD patients receiving everolimus showed ≥5-point PARS decrease vs. 1/8 placebo.

title: Beneficial Effects of Everolimus on Autism and Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder Symptoms in a Group of Patients with Tuberous Sclerosis Complex
journal: Journal of Child and Adolescent Psychopharmacology
abstract_summary: Retrospective study examining everolimus effects on autism and ADHD symptoms in TSC patients. Significant improvements in ABC (Aberrant Behavior Checklist) subscales and SRS (Social Responsiveness Scale) scores were observed, suggesting mTOR inhibition addresses neuropsychiatric manifestations of TSC beyond seizure control.
mechanism_of_action: mTOR inhibitor that corrects hyperactivated PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway in TSC, restoring normal neuronal protein synthesis, synaptic plasticity, and reducing neuroinflammation; also improves iron/copper homeostasis (via ceruloplasmin and transferrin elevation) linked to antioxidant improvements
sub_mechanism: mTORC1 complex inhibition; homeostatic correction of oxidant/antioxidant status
target_symptoms: Autism behaviors, ADHD symptoms, social impairment
results_summary: Significant beneficial effects on autism and ADHD symptoms as measured by ABC and SRS scales in TSC patients treated with everolimus.

title: Rapamycin for treating Tuberous sclerosis and Autism spectrum disorders
abstract_summary: Review article examining rapamycin's therapeutic potential for TSC-related ASD. Discusses how TSC1/TSC2 mutations cause mTOR hyperactivation associated with ASD in 20-60% of TSC cases, and that altered TSC/mTOR signaling appears as a common feature in a subset of non-syndromic ASD cases, expanding the potential therapeutic scope.
mechanism_of_action: Allosteric mTORC1 inhibitor that binds FKBP12 and directly inhibits mTOR kinase; reduces synaptic protein synthesis dysregulation, corrects neuronal hypertrophy and cortical tuber formation, and normalizes autophagic flux
sub_mechanism: Allosteric mTORC1 inhibition; synaptic protein synthesis normalization
target_symptoms: Core autism symptoms, cognitive impairments, seizures in TSC
results_summary: Review concludes rapamycin and related compounds show significant promise; preclinical models demonstrate behavioral, cognitive, and neuropathological improvement; clinical implications may extend beyond TSC to a broader subset of ASD.

title: Rapamycin improves sociability in the BTBR T+Itpr3tf/J mouse model of autism spectrum disorders
abstract_summary: Demonstrated that chronic rapamycin (6 mg/kg) treatment significantly improved sociability in the BTBR mouse model of idiopathic ASD. This is notable because BTBR mice do not carry TSC mutations, suggesting mTOR dysregulation plays a broader role in non-syndromic ASD pathophysiology.
mechanism_of_action: mTORC1 inhibition via FKBP12-rapamycin complex; corrects elevated mTOR activity implicated in synaptic dysregulation in idiopathic ASD models; modulates AMPA/NMDA receptor trafficking and dendritic protein synthesis
results_summary: Rapamycin improved sociability in BTBR mice, suggesting therapeutic potential for idiopathic ASD beyond TSC-specific presentations; chronic 6 mg/kg dosing was effective.

title: Sulforaphane treatment of autism spectrum disorder (ASD)
journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences (PNAS)
abstract_summary: Landmark randomized double-blind placebo-controlled trial of sulforaphane (derived from broccoli sprouts) in 29 young men with ASD over 18 weeks. Sulforaphane produced substantial improvements in ABC (34% improvement, p<0.001) and SRS (17% improvement, p=0.017) scores; improvements reversed upon discontinuation.
mechanism_of_action: Potent activator of the Nrf2/ARE pathway, upregulating antioxidant and cytoprotective genes (NQO1, HO-1, GST); inhibits NF-κB-mediated neuroinflammation; induces heat shock response proteins (HSP70, HSP27); reduces oxidative stress and mitochondrial dysfunction implicated in ASD
sub_mechanism: Nrf2/ARE pathway activation; heat shock protein induction; NF-κB inhibition
target_symptoms: Social interaction, abnormal behavior, verbal communication
results_summary: Significant improvements in ABC (34% improvement, p<0.001) and SRS (17%, p=0.017) scores; CGI-I showed significant improvement in social interaction, abnormal behavior, and verbal communication; scores reversed toward baseline upon discontinuation.

title: Efficacy of Sulforaphane in Treatment of Children with Autism Spectrum Disorder: A Randomized Double-Blind Placebo-Controlled Multi-center Trial
journal: Journal of Autism and Developmental Disorders
abstract_summary: Multi-center randomized double-blind placebo-controlled trial of sulforaphane in children with ASD. Significant improvements observed in social and behavioral outcome measures, providing multicenter replication of earlier findings with sulforaphane, though effect sizes varied across age groups.
mechanism_of_action: Nrf2 pathway activator; reduces oxidative stress, neuroinflammation, and mitochondrial dysfunction; upregulates cytoprotective genes including NQO1, HO-1; modulates multiple targets including AKT1, STAT1, MAPK1, MMP9, JAK2 according to network pharmacology analysis
sub_mechanism: Nrf2 activation; antioxidant response; anti-inflammatory via PI3K-Akt, mTOR, TNF, IL-17 pathways
results_summary: Significant improvements in ASD-related social and behavioral symptoms; multisite replication strengthens sulforaphane's evidence base as adjunctive therapy.

title: Investigating the clinical efficacy, safety and molecular mechanism of sulforaphane in autism spectrum disorder: an integrated study combining meta-analysis, network pharmacology, and computational biology
abstract_summary: Meta-analysis of 6 RCTs (333 participants) found both 4-5 week and 8-10 week sulforaphane supplementation significantly decreased Social Responsiveness Scale scores compared to placebo. Network pharmacology identified 10 core targets (AKT1, EGFR, HSP90AA1, SRC, CASP3, STAT1, MAPK1, MMP9, MAPK8, JAK2); Mendelian randomization revealed inverse association between STAT1 levels and ASD risk.
mechanism_of_action: Multi-target Nrf2 activator; network pharmacology confirms core targets AKT1, EGFR, HSP90AA1, SRC, CASP3, STAT1, MAPK1, MMP9, MAPK8, JAK2; mediates antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, autophagy-modulating, and cytoprotective effects; STAT1-ASD inverse association suggests immune regulation is key
sub_mechanism: Nrf2/ARE activation; multi-kinase modulation; autophagy induction
results_summary: Pooled meta-analysis shows sulforaphane significantly reduces SRS scores at both 4-5 weeks and 8-10 weeks; no significant difference in adverse events; considered safe and efficacious adjunctive therapy.

title: Immunoregulatory and/or Anti-inflammatory Agents for the Management of Core and Associated Symptoms in Individuals with Autism Spectrum Disorder: A Narrative Review of Randomized, Placebo-Controlled Trials
abstract_summary: Comprehensive narrative review of RCTs on immunoregulatory/anti-inflammatory agents for ASD over the last 10 years. Found beneficial effects of prednisolone, pregnenolone, celecoxib, and omega-3 on core symptoms including stereotyped behavior; add-on treatment with these agents plus minocycline, NAC, and sulforaphane improved irritability, hyperactivity, and lethargy vs. placebo.
mechanism_of_action: Selective COX-2 inhibitor that reduces prostaglandin E2 synthesis and neuroinflammation; inhibits microglial activation and pro-inflammatory cytokine release (IL-6, IL-17A); restores T-regulatory/T-helper-17 cell imbalances observed in ASD; COX-2 expressed in glutamatergic neurons may also modulate glutamate excitotoxicity
sub_mechanism: COX-2 inhibition; prostaglandin pathway suppression; microglial modulation
target_symptoms: Stereotyped behavior, irritability, hyperactivity, lethargy, social interaction
results_summary: Celecoxib as add-on to risperidone demonstrated significant improvement in irritability, stereotypy, and social withdrawal vs. risperidone alone in included trials; overall anti-inflammatory agents show benefit across multiple symptom domains.

title: Palmitoylethanolamide and Its Biobehavioral Correlates in Autism Spectrum Disorder: A Systematic Review of Human and Animal Evidence
abstract_summary: Systematic review of human and animal evidence for PEA in ASD. PEA acts via PPAR-α and cannabinoid CB2 receptor pathways to modulate neuroinflammation and endocannabinoid tone; children with ASD show lower endogenous PEA levels; case series show improvements in language, cognition, and social behaviors.
mechanism_of_action: Endocannabinoid-like lipid mediator that activates PPAR-α (anti-inflammatory), CB2 receptors (mast cell suppression), and TRPV1 receptors (entourage effect); inhibits NF-κB and mast cell degranulation; reduces neuroinflammatory cytokines; co-ultramicronization with luteolin enhances neuroprotective/antioxidant effects
sub_mechanism: PPAR-α activation; CB2 receptor agonism; mast cell stabilization; NF-κB inhibition
target_symptoms: Social behavior, language, cognition, neuroinflammation-related symptoms
results_summary: Lower endogenous PEA levels in ASD children; case series show improvements in expressive language, cognition, and behaviors; animal models show anti-inflammatory and prosocial effects; formal RCTs are still needed.

title: The Supplementation Therapy in Autism and Response to Treatment (START) Study: An Open-Label Feasibility Trial of Ultramicronized Palmitoylethanolamide in Autistic Adults
abstract_summary: First open-label feasibility trial of ultramicronized PEA in adults with Level 1 ASD, assessing 12-week treatment for psychic distress (anxiety, depression). The study evaluates PEA's effects on neuroinflammation biomarkers, metabolic profile, endocannabinoid system modulation, and microbial composition as potential therapeutic mechanisms.
mechanism_of_action: Ultramicronized formulation improves oral bioavailability; PPAR-α mediated anti-neuroinflammatory effects; endocannabinoid system modulation corrects the lower AEA, PEA, OEA tone documented in ASD; gut microbiome modulation via anti-inflammatory metabolite generation
sub_mechanism: PPAR-α activation; endocannabinoid system normalization; gut-brain axis modulation
target_symptoms: Anxiety, depression, psychic distress, ASD core symptoms
results_summary: Feasibility data published; study investigates um-PEA's effects on psychic distress in adults with Level 1 ASD; results will assess neuroinflammatory markers, eCB system, and microbiome as therapeutic mechanisms.

title: Cannabinoid treatment for autism: a proof-of-concept randomized trial
abstract_summary: Proof-of-concept RCT comparing whole-plant cannabis extract (CBD:THC 20:1), purified CBD, and placebo in children with ASD with disruptive behavior. Whole-plant extract showed significant improvement on Home Situation Questionnaire-ASD (9.5-point decrease vs. 3.6-point for placebo, p=0.009); no serious adverse events; common adverse events included somnolence.
mechanism_of_action: CBD modulates endocannabinoid system via CB1/CB2 receptors, TRPV1, GPR55, 5-HT1A; restores deficient endocannabinoid tone (AEA, PEA, OEA lower in ASD); normalizes glutamate-GABA balance; reduces neuroinflammation via CB2 and TRP channel-mediated effects; CBD:THC synergy (entourage effect) may enhance benefits
sub_mechanism: Endocannabinoid system restoration; CB1/CB2 modulation; GABA/glutamate balance normalization
target_symptoms: Disruptive behavior, social responsiveness, anxiety, repetitive behaviors
results_summary: Whole-plant extract (CBD:THC 20:1) significantly improved disruptive behavior (HSQ-ASD -9.5 vs. -3.6 placebo, p=0.009); purified CBD did not reach significance; no serious adverse events.

title: Evaluation of the efficacy and safety of cannabidiol-rich cannabis extract in children with autism spectrum disorder: randomized, double-blind, and placebo-controlled clinical trial
abstract_summary: RCT of CBD-rich cannabis extract in 60 children with ASD aged 5-11 years over 12 weeks. Significant improvements in social interaction (p=0.0002), anxiety (p=0.016), psychomotor agitation (p=0.003), number of meals per day (p=0.04), and concentration in mild ASD (p=0.01); only 3 children experienced mild adverse effects.
mechanism_of_action: Multi-target endocannabinoid modulation: CB1/CB2 agonism reduces hyperactivated neural circuits; anxiolytic effects via 5-HT1A partial agonism; anti-neuroinflammatory via CB2-mediated microglial modulation; TRPV1 activation reduces sensitization; restores AEA and 2-AG deficiency in ASD
sub_mechanism: CB1/CB2 agonism; 5-HT1A partial agonism; TRPV1 modulation; anti-neuroinflammatory
target_symptoms: Social interaction, anxiety, psychomotor agitation, concentration
results_summary: Significant improvements in social interaction, anxiety, agitation, and concentration; 9.7% adverse events (mild: dizziness, insomnia, colic, weight gain); CBD-rich extract safely improves core ASD diagnostic criterion of social interaction.

title: Purified cannabidiol leads to improvement of severe treatment-resistant behavioral symptoms in children with autism spectrum disorder
abstract_summary: Prospective observational before-and-after study of purified CBD as add-on in 20 children with severe ASD and intellectual disability unresponsive to conventional treatment. At 11-month follow-up, 18/20 (90%) showed improvement in at least one symptom; approximately 30% improvement in irritability, social withdrawal, and hyperactivity; 40% of children could reduce or discontinue concomitant medications.
mechanism_of_action: Purified CBD (no THC) modulates endocannabinoid system; FDA-approved formulation (Epidiolex-class); reduces neuroinflammation and oxidative stress; modulates glutamate-GABA systems differently in ASD vs. neurotypical brains (prefrontal GABA decrease vs. increase in controls); EEG aperiodic signal changes correlate with CBD metabolite levels
sub_mechanism: Endocannabinoid modulation; GABA/glutamate balance; aperiodic neural activity normalization
target_symptoms: Repetitive behaviors, irritability, social withdrawal, hyperactivity, sleep
results_summary: 90% of patients improved in at least one symptom; 83.5% of reported symptoms improved; 30% improvement in irritability, social withdrawal, hyperactivity; 40% reduced/discontinued concomitant medications; adverse effects mainly mild increased irritability/decreased appetite.

title: Long-term benefit of Microbiota Transfer Therapy on autism symptoms and gut microbiota
abstract_summary: Two-year follow-up of 18 ASD children who completed an open-label MTT trial (antibiotics + bowel cleanse + extended FMT). GI symptom improvements were maintained two years post-treatment; ASD behavioral symptoms improved even more than at end of treatment; increases in bacterial diversity and Bifidobacteria/Prevotella abundances were maintained.
mechanism_of_action: Intensive FMT protocol combining vancomycin pretreatment (to reduce Clostridial/dysbiotic bacteria), bowel cleanse, then high-dose fecal microbiota transplant followed by daily maintenance doses; restores healthy microbial diversity; increases Bifidobacterium, Prevotella; reduces Desulfovibrio; normalizes metabolite profiles (SCFAs, tryptophan, p-cresol sulfate) influencing the gut-brain axis
sub_mechanism: Gut microbiome restoration; metabolite normalization; gut-brain axis correction
target_symptoms: GI symptoms, ASD core behavioral symptoms, social function
results_summary: GI improvements maintained at 2-year follow-up; ASD symptoms improved further post-treatment (CARS, ATEC scores); bacterial diversity increases persisted; Bifidobacteria and Prevotella abundance maintained at higher levels.

title: Microbiota Transfer Therapy alters gut ecosystem and improves gastrointestinal and autism symptoms: an open-label study
abstract_summary: Pioneering open-label clinical trial of MTT in 18 ASD children with GI symptoms. MTT involved 2-week vancomycin, bowel cleanse, then extended FMT over 7-8 weeks. Approximately 80% reduction in GI symptoms; ASD behavioral symptoms improved significantly; 8-week post-treatment data showed improvements persisted; partial engraftment of donor microbiota confirmed.
mechanism_of_action: Vancomycin eliminates Gram-positive dysbiotic bacteria (Clostridiales) that produce neurotoxic metabolites (p-cresol, HPHPA) affecting dopamine metabolism and behavior; subsequent FMT restores healthy microbiome diversity; Bifidobacterium and Prevotella increase supports SCFA production and tryptophan/serotonin metabolism; gut-brain axis normalization reduces neuroinflammation
sub_mechanism: Antibiotic-mediated dysbiosis reduction; FMT microbiome restoration; SCFA/neurotransmitter precursor normalization
target_symptoms: GI symptoms (constipation, diarrhea, abdominal pain), ASD behavioral symptoms
results_summary: ~80% reduction in GI symptoms (GSRS scores); significant improvement in ASD behavioral symptoms (PARS, ATEC, CARS); improvements persisted 8 weeks post-treatment; partial donor microbiota engraftment confirmed by sequencing.

title: Gut to brain interaction in Autism Spectrum Disorders: a randomized controlled trial on the role of probiotics on clinical, biochemical and neurophysiological parameters
abstract_summary: RCT examining probiotic supplementation in children with ASD, assessing both clinical behavioral outcomes and biochemical/neurophysiological parameters. The trial aimed to test whether restoring gut microbiota via probiotics could improve ASD symptoms through gut-brain axis normalization.
mechanism_of_action: Multi-strain probiotic supplementation restores gut microbial diversity; increases SCFA production (butyrate, propionate) which cross the blood-brain barrier to modulate epigenetics and neuroinflammation; normalizes serotonin and GABA precursor availability via tryptophan metabolism; reduces intestinal permeability ('leaky gut') and subsequent systemic inflammation
sub_mechanism: Microbiome restoration; SCFA production; gut barrier integrity; tryptophan-serotonin metabolism normalization
target_symptoms: ASD behavioral symptoms, GI symptoms, biochemical markers
results_summary: Trial examined clinical, biochemical, and neurophysiological effects of probiotic supplementation; provided mechanistic data on gut-brain connection in ASD.

title: Results of a phase Ib study of SB-121, an investigational probiotic formulation, a randomized controlled trial in participants with autism spectrum disorder
abstract_summary: Phase 1b randomized placebo-controlled double-blind crossover trial of SB-121, a probiotic formulation, in 15 participants with ASD. SB-121 significantly improved Vineland-3 Adaptive Behavior Composite score (p=0.03) and showed a trend for increased social/geometric viewing ratio on eye tracking; well tolerated with no serious adverse events.
mechanism_of_action: Specific probiotic strain combination targeting gut microbiome restoration; proposed mechanisms include microglial modulation via SCFA production, reduction of LPS-mediated neuroinflammation, and normalization of serotonin/dopamine precursor availability through tryptophan metabolism
sub_mechanism: Probiotic-mediated microbiome modulation; SCFA production; serotonin precursor normalization
results_summary: Significant improvement in Vineland-3 Adaptive Behavior Composite (p=0.03); trend for increased social viewing ratio; SB-121 safe and well-tolerated; supports further clinical evaluation.

title: A randomised controlled trial of vitamin D and omega-3 long chain polyunsaturated fatty acids in the treatment of irritability and hyperactivity among children with autism spectrum disorder
journal: Journal of Steroid Biochemistry and Molecular Biology
abstract_summary: 12-month randomized double-blind placebo-controlled VIDOMA trial in 111 NZ children with ASD testing vitamin D (2000 IU/day), omega-3 DHA (722 mg/day), or combination. Both omega-3 (-5.0 ABC irritability points, p=0.001) and vitamin D (-4.0 points, p=0.01) reduced irritability vs. placebo; vitamin D also significantly reduced hyperactivity vs. placebo.
mechanism_of_action: Vitamin D: VDR-mediated gene expression affecting serotonin synthesis (TPH2), oxytocin, neurotrophin production; anti-inflammatory via reducing IL-6, TNF-α; important for GABAergic neuron development. Omega-3 DHA: membrane fluidity normalization; PPAR-γ activation; anti-inflammatory eicosanoid production; enhances serotonin and dopamine neurotransmission; improves synaptic plasticity
sub_mechanism: VDR-mediated neuroprotection; PPAR-γ activation; membrane lipid normalization; serotonin pathway modulation
results_summary: Omega-3 reduced irritability significantly (p=0.001); vitamin D reduced irritability (p=0.01) and hyperactivity (p=0.047) vs. placebo; combination did not show additive benefit over individual supplements in this sample.

title: Cerebral Folate Deficiency, Folate Receptor Alpha Autoantibodies and Leucovorin (Folinic Acid) Treatment in Autism Spectrum Disorders: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
abstract_summary: Systematic review and meta-analysis examining folate receptor alpha autoantibodies (FRAAs) and leucovorin treatment in ASD. Pooled FRAA prevalence in ASD was 71% (19x higher than typical children); leucovorin improved overall ASD symptoms (67%), communication (medium-large effect sizes), irritability (58%), and epilepsy (75%); well-tolerated with mild adverse effects.
mechanism_of_action: Reduced folate form that bypasses folate receptor alpha (FRα) blockade by using the reduced folate carrier (RFC) as an alternative transport pathway; corrects cerebral folate deficiency (CFD) caused by FRα autoantibodies (FRAAs) blocking 5-MTHF transport across the blood-brain barrier; restores adequate folate for methylation, purine synthesis, and neurotransmitter production in the CNS
sub_mechanism: Folate receptor alpha autoantibody bypass; cerebral folate deficiency correction; methyl cycle restoration
target_symptoms: Communication, core ASD symptoms, attention, stereotypy, irritability, epilepsy
results_summary: 71% prevalence of FRAAs in ASD (vs. ~4% in controls); leucovorin improved ASD symptoms overall (67%), communication with medium-large effect sizes, irritability (58%), ataxia (88%), and epilepsy (75%); evidence strongest from blinded placebo-controlled studies.

title: Cerebral folate receptor autoantibodies in autism spectrum disorder
abstract_summary: Landmark study measuring serum FRAs in 93 children with ASD finding 75.3% prevalence; FRA concentration correlated with CSF 5-MTHF (below normative mean in all measured cases); leucovorin treatment (2 mg/kg/day) significantly improved verbal communication, receptive/expressive language, attention, and stereotypy vs. wait-list controls; ~1/3 showed moderate to much improvement.
mechanism_of_action: Bypasses FRA blockade of blood-brain barrier folate transport via RFC; restores CSF 5-MTHF levels needed for neurotransmitter synthesis (serotonin, dopamine, norepinephrine via BH4), methylation reactions, DNA repair, and purine biosynthesis; corrects the specific CFD caused by FRA-mediated transport blockade
sub_mechanism: Folate receptor autoimmunity bypass; BH4 synthesis restoration; methylation and neurotransmitter pathway correction
study_type: Prospective cohort study with wait-list controlled treatment group
target_symptoms: Verbal communication, receptive/expressive language, attention, stereotypy
results_summary: 75.3% FRA prevalence in ASD; FRA+ children treated with leucovorin showed significant improvements in verbal communication, language, attention, and stereotypy vs. wait-list controls (p significant); ~1/3 showed moderate to much improvement; adverse effects low.

title: Melatonin: From Pharmacokinetics to Clinical Use in Autism Spectrum Disorder
abstract_summary: Comprehensive pharmacokinetic and clinical review of melatonin in ASD. Reduced melatonin secretion is well-documented in ASD; multiple clinical trials demonstrate benefits for sleep latency and quality; discusses formulation differences (immediate vs. prolonged-release) and the complex role of melatonin beyond sleep, including antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and neuroprotective effects.
mechanism_of_action: MT1/MT2 receptor agonist regulating circadian rhythm and sleep architecture; ASMT (HIOMT) enzyme deficiency documented in ASD impairs melatonin synthesis; also acts as potent antioxidant scavenging free radicals; anti-inflammatory effects via NF-κB inhibition; neuroprotective via mitochondrial stabilization; modulates immune system and GABAergic/glutamatergic signaling
sub_mechanism: MT1/MT2 agonism; circadian rhythm normalization; antioxidant/anti-inflammatory neuroprotection
target_symptoms: Sleep onset latency, sleep duration, sleep quality, circadian rhythm disturbances
results_summary: Multiple RCTs demonstrate significant reductions in sleep onset latency and improvements in sleep quality in ASD; prolonged-release formulations show sustained benefits; melatonin well-tolerated with minimal adverse effects in ASD populations.

title: Low-dose suramin in autism spectrum disorder: a small, phase I/II, randomized clinical trial
journal: Annals of Clinical and Translational Neurology
abstract_summary: The SAT-1 (Suramin Autism Treatment-1) trial: first phase I/II double-blind placebo-controlled translational pilot study of low-dose suramin IV infusion in children with ASD. 5 children received a single suramin infusion (20 mg/kg); significant improvements in core ASD symptoms across all 5 suramin-treated vs. 0/5 placebo at 6 weeks; improvements included language, social interaction, and focus.
mechanism_of_action: Broad-spectrum purinergic signaling antagonist (blocks P2X and P2Y receptor families); interrupts the cell danger response (CDR) cycle that maintains a chronic hypometabolic state in ASD; corrects mitochondrial function by reducing ATP/ADP release signaling; inhibits multiple kinases and growth factor receptors; normalizes purine metabolism dysregulation
sub_mechanism: Antipurinergic therapy (APT); purinergic P2 receptor antagonism; cell danger response interruption; mitochondrial function restoration
study_type: Phase I/II randomized double-blind placebo-controlled pilot trial
target_symptoms: Core ASD symptoms: language, social interaction, focus, repetitive behaviors
results_summary: All 5 suramin-treated children showed significant improvement in core ASD symptoms at 6 weeks vs. 0/5 placebo; improvements in language, social interaction, and restricted/repetitive behaviors; treatment was well tolerated at 20 mg/kg single-dose.

title: Randomized clinical trial of low dose suramin intravenous infusions for treatment of autism spectrum disorder
abstract_summary: Phase 2 randomized controlled trial of low-dose suramin (PAX-101/AST-001) intravenous infusions in children with ASD conducted by PaxMedica Inc. The trial extended SAT-1 findings with repeated infusion design, assessing safety and efficacy of suramin as an antipurinergic therapy for core ASD symptoms targeting P2X receptor-mediated neuro-inflammation and mitochondrial dysfunction.
mechanism_of_action: Antagonism of purinergic P2X receptors (P2X1-7) and P2Y receptors; reduces neuroinflammation via interrupting ATP/UTP danger signaling; restores normal mitochondrial function by correcting the purinergic-mediated 'cell danger response'; corrects dysregulated purine metabolism documented in metabolomic studies of ASD
sub_mechanism: P2X purinergic receptor antagonism; cell danger response (CDR) modulation; neuroinflammation reduction
target_symptoms: Core ASD symptoms, social communication, repetitive behaviors
results_summary: Phase 2 trial data provided further evidence for suramin's activity; antipurinergic mechanism confirmed; specific efficacy outcomes published by PaxMedica as Phase 2 results.

title: A randomized placebo-controlled pilot trial shows that intranasal vasopressin improves social deficits in children with autism
abstract_summary: Landmark 4-week double-blind randomized placebo-controlled trial of intranasal arginine vasopressin (AVP) in 30 children with ASD. AVP significantly improved SRS-2 primary outcome (F1,20=9.853, p=0.0052, Cohen's d=1.40); reduced anxiety and repetitive behaviors; no serious adverse events; effects more pronounced when accounting for pretreatment blood AVP concentrations.
mechanism_of_action: Exogenous AVP delivery corrects deficient central vasopressin tone documented in ASD (lower CSF vasopressin in socially impaired individuals); V1a receptor activation in amygdala, lateral septum, and social brain circuits promotes social recognition, social bonding, and affiliative behaviors; reduces anxiety via V1a-mediated HPA axis modulation; CSF AVP levels inversely correlate with ASD severity
sub_mechanism: V1a/V1b vasopressin receptor agonism; social brain circuit activation; HPA axis modulation
results_summary: AVP significantly improved SRS-2 primary outcome (p=0.0052, Cohen's d=1.40); reduced anxiety and some repetitive behaviors; 0 dropouts in AVP group; no significant adverse events or vital sign changes; effects moderated by baseline AVP blood concentration.

title: A pilot controlled trial of insulin-like growth factor-1 in children with Phelan-McDermid syndrome
abstract_summary: First controlled trial of IGF-1 in children with Phelan-McDermid syndrome (SHANK3 deletion) and ASD. 9 children in 12-week double-blind crossover design; IGF-1 phase showed significant improvement in social impairment and restricted/repetitive behaviors vs. placebo (ABC and RBS scales); IGF-1 well tolerated with no serious adverse events.
mechanism_of_action: Neurotrophic polypeptide that promotes AMPA receptor insertion at postsynaptic densities via PI3K/AKT/mTOR signaling; reverses SHANK3-deficiency-induced loss of postsynaptic scaffolding and synaptic hypofunction; promotes neuronal survival, dendritic arborization, and myelination; rescues excitatory/inhibitory synapse balance; also shown to improve breathing, motor, and behavioral abnormalities in Rett syndrome models via similar mechanisms
sub_mechanism: IGF-1R/PI3K/AKT signaling; AMPA receptor trafficking; synaptic scaffolding restoration; SHANK3-pathway rescue
results_summary: Significant improvement in social impairment and restrictive behaviors during IGF-1 phase vs. placebo (ABC and RBS measures); well tolerated, no serious adverse events; proof-of-concept for synaptic targeted IGF-1 therapy in SHANK3-related ASD.

title: Safety, pharmacokinetics, and preliminary assessment of efficacy of mecasermin (recombinant human IGF-1) for the treatment of Rett syndrome
journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences
abstract_summary: Phase I open-label trial of mecasermin (recombinant human IGF-1) in girls with Rett syndrome. 9 participants, 20-week treatment; demonstrated safety and tolerability; preliminary improvements in breathing (apnea score) and behavioral measures; provided mechanistic and safety data for IGF-1 in RTT, informing subsequent controlled trials.
mechanism_of_action: IGF-1 receptor-mediated signaling via PI3K/AKT: in RTT (MECP2 mutations), rescues BDNF deficit, promotes PSD-95 and AMPA receptor expression, restores synaptic connectivity and excitatory/inhibitory balance; improves neuronal survival and myelination; IGF-1 active N-terminal tripeptide (GPE) is the basis for trofinetide development
sub_mechanism: IGF-1R/PI3K/AKT signaling; MECP2-pathway downstream rescue; BDNF upregulation; synaptic E/I balance restoration
target_symptoms: Breathing irregularities, motor function, behavioral measures, ASD-related symptoms in RTT
results_summary: Demonstrated safety and tolerability; improvements in apnea frequency and behavioral measures; no serious adverse events; supported advancement to controlled phase 2 trials; provided critical PK data.

title: Trofinetide for the treatment of Rett syndrome: a randomized phase 3 study
abstract_summary: Pivotal phase 3 LAVENDER trial (NCT04181723) of trofinetide in 187 females with Rett syndrome. Trofinetide vs. placebo showed significant improvement in RSBQ (-4.9 vs. -1.7, p=0.0175, Cohen's d=0.37) and CGI-I (3.5 vs. 3.8, p=0.0030, Cohen's d=0.47). These results led to FDA approval as DAYBUE™, the first-ever approved treatment for RTT.
mechanism_of_action: Synthetic peptidase-resistant analog of glycine-proline-glutamate (GPE, N-terminal IGF-1 tripeptide); differs from GPE by methyl substitution at proline α-carbon improving oral bioavailability; modulates NMDA receptors (contains glutamate and glycine) to normalize neuronal activity; anti-inflammatory via IGF-1 pathway activation; promotes synaptic connectivity and reduces dendritic spine abnormalities caused by MECP2 mutations
sub_mechanism: IGF-1 pathway activation (GPE analog); NMDA receptor modulation; synaptic connectivity restoration; MECP2-downstream pathway rescue
target_symptoms: Core Rett syndrome symptoms (communication, motor, behavior), global clinical impression
results_summary: Significant RSBQ improvement (p=0.0175, d=0.37); significant CGI-I improvement (p=0.0030, d=0.47); FDA approved March 2023 as DAYBUE; most common adverse effect was diarrhea; first FDA-approved treatment for RTT.

title: Double-blind, randomized, placebo-controlled study of trofinetide in pediatric Rett syndrome
abstract_summary: Phase 2 RCT of trofinetide in pediatric Rett syndrome patients. Clinically relevant improvements vs. placebo on Rett Syndrome Behaviour Questionnaire, RTT-Clinician Specific Concerns-Visual Analog Scale, and CGI-Improvement scale; improvements correlated with trofinetide exposure (PK-PD relationship confirmed); supported advancement to phase 3.
mechanism_of_action: GPE analog activating IGF-1/PI3K downstream signaling; NMDA receptor modulation via glycine and glutamate components; corrects dendritic spine hyperdensity and synaptic dysfunction in MECP2-mutant neurons; reduces reactive gliosis and neuroinflammation; exposure-response relationship for behavioral improvement confirmed
sub_mechanism: GPE/IGF-1 pathway; NMDA modulation; anti-neuroinflammatory; synaptic plasticity normalization
target_symptoms: Rett syndrome core symptoms, behavioral questionnaire, clinician-specific concerns
results_summary: Clinically meaningful improvements on RSBQ, RTT-CSVAS, and CGI-I vs. placebo; improvements correlated with drug exposure; well tolerated; enabled phase 3 design.

title: Emerging drugs for the treatment of irritability associated with autism spectrum disorder
abstract_summary: Comprehensive review of emerging pharmacotherapies for ASD-associated irritability based on RCT evidence. Identifies multiple agents beyond risperidone/aripiprazole: memantine, N-acetylcysteine, galantamine, sulforaphane, celecoxib, palmitoylethanolamide, pentoxifylline, simvastatin, minocycline, amantadine, pregnenolone, prednisolone, riluzole, propentofylline, pioglitazone, and topiramate all outperformed placebo as adjuncts to risperidone; mechanisms span glutamatergic, GABAergic, inflammatory, oxidative, cholinergic, dopaminergic, and serotonergic systems.
drug_name: Multiple emerging drugs (minocycline, celecoxib, PEA, memantine, NAC)
mechanism_of_action: Minocycline: tetracycline antibiotic with potent microglial inhibition and anti-neuroinflammatory effects; inhibits matrix metalloproteinases (MMPs), reduces TNF-α/IL-1β/IL-6 release from activated microglia; chelates iron reducing Fenton reaction; MAPK pathway inhibition. Celecoxib: selective COX-2 inhibition. PEA: PPAR-α/CB2 agonism. Memantine: NMDA antagonism for excitotoxicity reduction.
sub_mechanism: Microglial inhibition (minocycline); COX-2 inhibition (celecoxib); PPAR-α (PEA); NMDA antagonism (memantine)
results_summary: Multiple emerging agents showed significant improvement in irritability vs. placebo as adjuncts to risperidone; all reported safe and tolerable; further studies with larger samples and longer follow-up needed.

title: Effects of cannabidiol on brain excitation and inhibition systems; a randomised placebo-controlled single dose trial during magnetic resonance spectroscopy in adults with and without autism spectrum disorder
abstract_summary: MRS-based randomized crossover study of single 600mg CBD dose in 34 men (17 ASD, 17 neurotypical) showing CBD differentially modulates brain GABA+ and Glx (glutamate+glutamine). Critically, CBD decreased GABA+ in ASD prefrontal cortex but increased it in controls, suggesting different pharmacological response; CBD increased subcortical Glx across groups.
mechanism_of_action: CBD modulates glutamate-GABA systems via multiple mechanisms: allosteric GABA-A positive modulator, TRPV1 agonism reducing glutamate release, GPR55 antagonism; in ASD specifically, the prefrontal GABA response is reversed vs. neurotypicals, reflecting differential endocannabinoid-GABAergic coupling; CBD's 5-HT1A partial agonism may contribute differently in ASD-specific neural circuits
sub_mechanism: GABA-A modulation; TRPV1-mediated glutamate reduction; 5-HT1A partial agonism; differential ASD-specific pharmacology
study_type: Randomized double-blind placebo-controlled crossover MRS study
target_symptoms: Brain glutamate-GABA balance as biomarker proxy
results_summary: CBD increased GABA+ in controls but decreased it in ASD (DMPFC p<significant for group difference); CBD increased subcortical Glx across groups; demonstrates fundamental pharmacological difference in CBD effects in ASD vs. typical brains; supports ASD-specific CBD trial design.

title: The effect of vitamin D supplementation in treatment of children with autism spectrum disorder: a systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials
abstract_summary: Systematic review and meta-analysis of 5 RCTs (349 children) examining vitamin D supplementation in ASD. Pooled analysis showed significant improvements in ASD severity scales; vitamin D supplementation appeared beneficial for behavioral and neurological symptoms in children with ASD, consistent with VDR-mediated neurodevelopmental effects.
mechanism_of_action: Vitamin D receptor (VDR)-mediated genome-wide effects including upregulation of serotonin synthesis (TPH2 in brain), oxytocin gene expression, and neurotrophin production (BDNF, NGF); anti-inflammatory via inhibiting pro-inflammatory cytokine production; regulates GABAergic neuron development; VDR expressed throughout brain including hippocampus, cerebellum, prefrontal cortex; widespread vitamin D deficiency documented in ASD
sub_mechanism: VDR-mediated neuroprotection; serotonin/oxytocin gene regulation; BDNF upregulation; anti-inflammatory cytokine modulation
study_type: Systematic review and meta-analysis of RCTs
target_symptoms: ASD severity, behavioral symptoms, social communication
results_summary: Pooled RCT data shows significant improvements in ASD symptom severity with vitamin D supplementation; consistent evidence across 5 trials; optimal dosing, duration, and baseline vitamin D status for treatment response need further investigation.

title: Distinct Fecal and Plasma Metabolites in Children with Autism Spectrum Disorders and Their Modulation after Microbiota Transfer Therapy
abstract_summary: Comprehensive metabolomic analysis of 619 plasma and 669 fecal metabolites in ASD children before and after MTT. ASD children had significantly different plasma metabolite profiles (lower nicotinamide riboside, IMP, and 8 other metabolites; higher caprylate and heptanoate). MTT drove global shifts in plasma metabolites toward typically-developing levels, demonstrating gut-brain metabolic connection.
mechanism_of_action: MTT corrects ASD-associated metabolic dysregulation: normalizes purine metabolism (IMP/nicotinamide riboside pathways), reduces elevated short-chain fatty acids (caprylate, heptanoate) linked to mitochondrial dysfunction, reduces p-cresol sulfate (neurotoxic Clostridial metabolite), and shifts global plasma metabolome toward typically-developing profile; demonstrates microbiome-metabolome-brain axis
sub_mechanism: Purine metabolism normalization; mitochondrial metabolite correction; toxic microbial metabolite reduction; systemic metabolome normalization
study_type: Metabolomic analysis of open-label clinical trial samples
target_symptoms: ASD-associated metabolic dysregulation, GI symptoms, behavioral symptoms
results_summary: MTT normalized multiple dysregulated plasma metabolites in ASD toward typically-developing levels; p-cresol sulfate (neurotoxic) decreased; global metabolomic shifts support gut-brain metabolic mechanism; provides biomarker candidates for future controlled trials.

title: Clinical trial of insulin-like growth factor-1 in Phelan-McDermid syndrome
abstract_summary: Follow-up RCT of IGF-1 in 10 children with PMS using 12-week double-blind crossover design. Primary ABC-Social Withdrawal endpoint did not reach significance alone, but significant improvements were found in sensory reactivity symptoms; pooled analysis (N=19 with prior trial) showed significant improvements in repetitive behaviors and hyperactivity; IGF-1 well tolerated.
mechanism_of_action: IGF-1 receptor signaling via PI3K/AKT/mTOR restores SHANK3-deficiency-related synaptic dysfunction; increases PSD-95 and glutamate receptor expression; promotes AMPA receptor trafficking; reduces AMPA receptor hypofunction that characterizes PMS; also reduces neuroinflammatory markers and promotes axon myelination
sub_mechanism: IGF-1R synaptic rescue; AMPA/NMDA receptor expression restoration; SHANK3 downstream pathway correction
target_symptoms: Social withdrawal, sensory reactivity, repetitive behaviors, hyperactivity
results_summary: Significant improvements in sensory reactivity; pooled analysis (N=19) showed significant improvements in repetitive behaviors and hyperactivity; primary ABC-SW endpoint not significant; IGF-1 well tolerated, no serious adverse events.

title: Lower circulating endocannabinoid levels in children with autism spectrum disorder
abstract_summary: Measured serum endocannabinoids in 93 children with ASD vs. typical controls finding significantly lower AEA (anandamide), PEA, and OEA levels in ASD. Provides biochemical rationale for cannabinoid-based therapies in ASD: deficient endocannabinoid tone correlates with social and behavioral symptoms; PEA and OEA are proposed as both biomarkers and therapeutic targets.
mechanism_of_action: Documents endocannabinoid system deficiency in ASD: lower AEA (binds CB1/CB2/TRPV1), PEA (PPAR-α/CB2 agonist), and OEA (PPAR-α agonist) serum levels; provides biochemical basis for CBD/PEA therapeutic supplementation to restore deficient endocannabinoid tone; lower AEA correlates with more severe social impairment in animal ASD models
sub_mechanism: Endocannabinoid system deficiency biomarker; AEA/PEA/OEA tone quantification
results_summary: ASD children had significantly lower serum AEA, PEA, and OEA vs. controls; findings suggest deficient endocannabinoid tone in ASD; supports therapeutic rationale for cannabinoid/PEA supplementation; further studies needed to determine if CSF levels differ similarly.

title: Randomized controlled trial of sulforaphane and metabolite discovery in children with Autism Spectrum Disorder
abstract_summary: 15-week parallel randomized double-blind placebo-controlled trial of sulforaphane in 57 children aged 3-12 with ASD, followed by 15-week open-label phase. Primary OACIS outcome did not show significant between-group differences; however, secondary ABC measure showed significant caregiver improvement (Cohen's d=-0.96, p for ABC at 15 weeks); sulforaphane significantly altered biomarkers of glutathione redox status, mitochondrial respiration, and heat shock proteins.
mechanism_of_action: Nrf2/ARE activation improving glutathione redox status; induction of heat shock proteins (HSP70); improvement of mitochondrial respiration; reduction of inflammatory markers; specific biomarker changes confirm mechanism of action at cellular level; younger children (3-12) may show different response vs. older subjects from prior trial
sub_mechanism: Nrf2 activation; mitochondrial biogenesis; heat shock response; glutathione redox improvement
study_type: Randomized double-blind placebo-controlled trial with open-label extension
target_symptoms: General autism level (OACIS), aberrant behavior, social responsiveness
results_summary: Primary OACIS not significant; ABC significantly improved on caregiver ratings (Cohen's d=-0.96); significant mechanistic biomarker changes (glutathione, mitochondrial, heat shock proteins, inflammatory markers); ABC and SRS improved with length of SF exposure in non-randomized analysis.

title: Beneficial Effects of Co-Ultramicronized Palmitoylethanolamide/Luteolin in a Mouse Model of Autism and in a Case Report of Autism
abstract_summary: Animal and human case study demonstrating efficacy of co-ultramicronized PEA plus luteolin in ASD. In valproate-induced autism mouse model, PEA-luteolin reduced social deficits, repetitive behaviors, and neuroinflammation. In a human ASD case report, co-ultramicronization with luteolin produced significant behavioral improvements, supporting further controlled trials.
