mechanism_of_action: Synergistic neuroprotection: PEA activates PPAR-α and CB2 reducing neuroinflammation; luteolin is a flavonoid inhibiting NF-κB, reducing oxidative stress, modulating mast cell activation; co-ultramicronization enhances bioavailability and synergistic anti-inflammatory effects; combined reduction of TNF-α, IL-6, IL-1β, and restoration of BDNF-like behavior
sub_mechanism: PPAR-α/CB2 agonism (PEA) + NF-κB/mast cell inhibition (luteolin); combined neuroinflammation reduction
results_summary: Valproate mouse model: PEA-LUT reduced autism-like behaviors and neuroinflammation; human case: significant behavioral improvements with co-ultramicronized PEA-luteolin; supports controlled clinical trial design.

title: A Placebo-Controlled Trial of Cannabinoid Treatment for Disruptive Behavior in Children and Adolescents with Autism Spectrum Disorder: Effects on Sleep Parameters as Measured by the CSHQ
abstract_summary: Secondary analysis of the 150-patient RCT comparing whole-plant cannabis extract (CBD:THC 20:1), purified CBD, and placebo in ASD children, focusing on sleep outcomes. Both cannabinoid treatments showed benefits for sleep disturbances (assessed by CSHQ) which are highly prevalent in ASD; provides evidence for cannabinoid-mediated sleep improvement in ASD.
mechanism_of_action: Cannabinoids improve sleep in ASD via multiple mechanisms: CB1 receptor-mediated sleep architecture normalization (increased deep sleep, reduced wakefulness); anxiolytic effects reducing sleep-onset anxiety; 5-HT1A activation promoting melatonin release; anti-inflammatory reduction of pain/discomfort disrupting sleep; THC-component's direct sedative effect at low ratios
sub_mechanism: CB1-mediated sleep architecture normalization; anxiolytic/sedative effects; melatonin pathway support
study_type: Randomized double-blind placebo-controlled trial (sleep outcomes substudy)
target_symptoms: Sleep disturbances, insomnia, night waking in ASD
results_summary: Cannabinoid treatment associated with significant improvement in CSHQ sleep parameters vs. placebo; both whole-plant and purified CBD showed beneficial effects on the highly prevalent sleep disruptions in ASD.

title: Vasopressin as Possible Treatment Option in Autism Spectrum Disorder
abstract_summary: Comprehensive review of vasopressin's role in ASD pathophysiology and therapeutic potential. Vasopressinergic system dysregulation may underlie male prevalence of ASD; both vasopressin agonists AND V1a receptor antagonists (balovaptan, RG7713) have shown clinical activity; suggests only specific subgroups (stress-axis disturbances, males) would benefit from vasopressin-targeted therapy.
mechanism_of_action: AVP V1a receptors in lateral septum, amygdala, and bed nucleus of stria terminalis mediate social recognition, pair bonding, and territorial behavior; V1b receptors modulate HPA axis stress response; both agonism (intranasal AVP) and V1a antagonism (balovaptan) show clinical activity suggesting circuit-dependent effects; CSF AVP deficit documented in socially impaired subjects across multiple species
sub_mechanism: V1a receptor modulation (agonism and antagonism); social circuit activation; HPA axis stress modulation
target_symptoms: Social functioning, social cognition, anxiety, stress regulation
results_summary: Both intranasal AVP and V1a antagonist (balovaptan) show clinical activity in ASD trials; specific subgroups (stress-axis dysfunction, males) may benefit most; suggests vasopressin pathway is therapeutically relevant but requires personalized approach.

title: Antipurinergic Therapy Corrects the Autism-Like Features in the Poly(IC) Mouse Model
abstract_summary: Foundational preclinical study demonstrating that suramin (antipurinergic therapy, APT) corrected 16 multisystem abnormalities in the maternal immune activation (poly IC) mouse model of ASD. Core social deficits, sensorimotor abnormalities, and metabolic disturbances were all corrected, establishing purinergic signaling as a key mechanism in ASD pathophysiology and rationale for clinical trials.
mechanism_of_action: Suramin corrects the 'cell danger response' (CDR): when cells sense danger, mitochondria release ATP/UTP as extracellular danger signals activating P2 purinergic receptors; in ASD, chronic CDR activation maintains cellular hypometabolism and impairs normal synaptic development; suramin blocks purinergic P2X and P2Y receptors, interrupting the CDR cycle and allowing cellular machinery to resume normal development and function
sub_mechanism: Cell danger response (CDR) interruption; P2X/P2Y receptor antagonism; mitochondrial function restoration
target_symptoms: Social deficits, sensorimotor abnormalities, metabolic disturbances
results_summary: Suramin corrected all 16 measured ASD-like abnormalities in poly-IC model including social deficits, sensorimotor issues, and metabolic markers; established purinergic signaling/CDR as key mechanism and supported SAT-1 human trial design.

title: Binding Folate Receptor Alpha Autoantibody Is a Biomarker for Leucovorin Treatment Response in Autism Spectrum Disorder
abstract_summary: Retrospective cohort analysis of 110 ASD clinic patients examining folate receptor alpha autoantibody (FRAA) titers as biomarkers for leucovorin treatment response. Higher binding FRAA titers associated with greater baseline ABC-irritability; leucovorin improved most SRS subscales with higher FRAA titer predicting greater treatment response; sFBPs associated with more severe ASD.
mechanism_of_action: Binding FRAAs block FRα-mediated 5-MTHF transport across choroid plexus into CSF; higher binding FRAA titer = greater blockade = greater CFD severity; leucovorin bypasses FRα via RFC, restoring CNS folate; restored methylation supports SAM generation for epigenetic regulation, neurotransmitter synthesis, and myelin maintenance; FRAA titer serves as biomarker stratifying treatment response
sub_mechanism: FRAA-mediated CFD correction; RFC-mediated folate bypass; methylation pathway restoration; biomarker-guided precision therapy
results_summary: Higher binding FRAA titer associated with greater ABC-irritability baseline; leucovorin improved most SRS subscales; higher FRAA titers predicted greater leucovorin response; sFBPs predicted more severe ASD and leucovorin response; supports biomarker-guided treatment approach.

title: mTOR, a Potential Target to Treat Autism Spectrum Disorder
abstract_summary: Review of mTOR pathway dysregulation across multiple ASD-associated monogenic disorders including TSC, PTEN-hamartoma syndrome, neurofibromatosis 1, and fragile X syndrome. mTOR hyperactivity is a common mechanistic thread across syndromic ASD; mutations in TSC1, TSC2, PTEN, NF1, and FMR1 all converge on mTOR dysregulation, making mTOR a broadly relevant therapeutic target.
mechanism_of_action: mTOR integrates growth factor signaling (PI3K/AKT), energy sensing (AMPK), and amino acid availability; hyperactive mTOR in ASD disrupts synaptic protein synthesis, dendritic spine morphology, excitatory/inhibitory balance, and neuronal migration; TSC1/TSC2, PTEN, NF1, and FMR1 all regulate mTOR; inhibition normalizes these downstream effects
sub_mechanism: Convergent mTOR hyperactivation across syndromic ASD; PI3K/AKT/mTOR axis dysregulation
target_symptoms: Core ASD behaviors, cognition, E/I balance, synaptic function
results_summary: Comprehensive review demonstrating convergent mTOR dysregulation across multiple genetic ASD syndromes; mTOR inhibitors (rapamycin/everolimus) show promise across these conditions; idiopathic ASD may also have mTOR hyperactivation in a subset of cases.

title: The Effectiveness of Cobalamin (B12) Treatment for Autism Spectrum Disorder: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
abstract_summary: Systematic review collating studies using cobalamin (B12) as treatment in ASD. Most studies (83%) used subcutaneously injected methylcobalamin (mB12); multiple open-label and some controlled studies show improvements in glutathione redox capacity and clinical symptoms; 78% of parents in one study desired continuation after study conclusion; mB12 injections appeared superior to oral formulations.
mechanism_of_action: Active cobalamin cofactor for methionine synthase, restoring homocysteine-to-methionine methylation; increases SAM (S-adenosylmethionine) generation supporting genome-wide methylation reactions; enhances glutathione synthesis via improved cysteine availability (transsulfuration pathway); corrects documented ASD-specific deficits in methylation capacity (elevated homocysteine, low SAM/SAH ratio), reduced glutathione, and increased oxidative stress; subcutaneous injection bypasses GI absorption variability and maximizes CNS delivery
sub_mechanism: Methionine synthase cofactor; SAM/methylation capacity restoration; glutathione redox correction; transsulfuration pathway enhancement
target_symptoms: Core ASD behaviors, metabolic biomarkers (GSH, SAM, homocysteine), communication
results_summary: 83% of studies used injected mB12; studies demonstrate improvements in metabolic markers (glutathione redox, methylation) and clinical ASD symptoms; adverse events few, mild, and not significantly different from placebo; 78% parental preference for continuation; larger multicenter RCTs needed.

title: Randomized, Placebo-Controlled Trial of Methyl B12 for Children with Autism
journal: Journal of Child and Adolescent Psychopharmacology
abstract_summary: Randomized placebo-controlled trial of subcutaneous methylcobalamin (mB12) injections in children with ASD. The trial assessed effects on methylation biomarkers (SAM/SAH ratio, glutathione levels) and behavioral outcomes; provided controlled evidence for mB12 effects on metabolic and clinical outcomes in ASD children.
mechanism_of_action: Subcutaneous mB12 serves as direct methionine synthase cofactor bypassing absorption limitations; corrects the ASD-characteristic low SAM/SAH methylation ratio; improves glutathione redox status (GSH/GSSG ratio) by enhancing cysteine availability via methionine cycle/transsulfuration; addresses documented methylation capacity deficits in ASD that affect gene expression regulation, neurotransmitter synthesis, and oxidative stress response
sub_mechanism: SAM/methylation cycle correction; glutathione redox restoration; methionine synthase activation
target_symptoms: ASD behavioral symptoms, methylation biomarkers, glutathione/oxidative stress markers
results_summary: Controlled trial evaluating subcutaneous mB12 effects on ASD behavioral and metabolic outcomes; demonstrated relationship between methylation biomarker improvement and clinical response; safety profile acceptable.
